label: Open door
name: What's on your mind?
description: Undirected, casual, invites any topic without setting an agenda. MBS Kickstart Question.

label: Their agenda
name: What would you most like to talk about today?
description: Hands full agenda ownership to them. Pure person-centered opener — nothing is presupposed.

label: Real check-in
name: How are you — really?
description: 'Really' subverts the ritual. One word signals this is a different kind of conversation.

label: Energy read
name: What's been giving you energy lately?
description: Strengths-based, not scoped to work. Reveals what genuinely animates the person.

label: What to push on
name: What feels worth pushing on right now?
description: Growth-fitted opener. Surfaces what the report is actively choosing to develop, not what's drifting.

label: Since we spoke
name: What's been on your mind since we last spoke?
description: Acknowledges the relationship and the gap between conversations. Open and non-evaluative.

label: Useful to you
name: What would make this a really useful conversation for you?
description: Positions them as expert on their own needs. Research shows this dramatically improves engagement.

label: What's alive
name: What's been alive for you lately?
description: 'Alive' invites vitality and the whole person — broader than 'what's on your mind', no work scope.

label: The real version
name: Tell me about your week — the real version.
description: Kim Scott's 'care personally' made concrete. Signals that a performed answer won't cut it.

label: Bit of joy
name: What's brought you a bit of joy recently?
description: 'A bit of' removes pressure to perform happiness. PERMA positive emotion — activates openness.

label: Head space
name: What's been taking up the most space in your head this week?
description: Spatial metaphor is more honest than 'what's on your mind'. Weight implies it matters.

label: What you're carrying
name: What are you carrying into this conversation?
description: Acknowledges people are not clean slates. Non-evaluative — carrying could be burden or gift.

label: Curiosity
name: What's something you've been curious about lately?
description: Positions them as active and alive, not performing. Works as a natural growth-oriented opener.

label: Learning about yourself
name: What have you been learning about yourself recently?
description: Signals interest in them as a human, not just their output. Reflective, non-evaluative.

label: Bring it up
name: Is there something you've been wanting to bring up?
description: Amy Edmondson: explicit space for withheld speech is the most powerful safety-building move a leader can make.

label: How you're arriving
name: How are you arriving today?
description: Acknowledges people come from somewhere. Grounding, non-evaluative — makes space before the conversation starts.

label: Most like yourself
name: When have you felt most like yourself recently?
description: Authentic self-concept research. Identity-based, not performance-based. Deep without being intrusive.

label: Looking forward
name: Before we get into specifics, what's been most on your mind about where you're heading?
description: Growth-native opener. Forward-leaning, lets the report frame their own trajectory before manager introduces concerns.

label: Where you are
name: What would you want me to understand about where you are right now?
description: Rogers' empathic understanding made explicit. Positions the manager as a receiver, not evaluator.

label: Outside of work
name: What's been the best part of your world outside of work lately?
description: Explicitly non-work. Kim Scott's 'care personally' — shows genuine interest in the whole person.

label: Time is yours
name: This time is yours. What would you like to do with it?
description: The most honest version of 'I'm here for you'. Makes explicit that the 1:1 belongs to them.

label: Anything to cover
name: Before we start, is there anything specific you want to make sure we cover today?
description: Manager-led check that the report has agenda input before the prepared questions begin.
